steps.code: struct Color { int r; int g; };
steps.annotation: STRATEGY: pass by VALUE = function gets a COPY. Start variable table: track bg.r, bg.g, c.r
No & on parameter c. This means c is a separate copy of whatever is passed in. Changes to c do NOT affect the caller.
bg.r = 255, bg.g = 128. Write these in your variable table.
bg is COPIED into c. c.r starts as 255. Inside fade: c.r = 0. But c is a copy! bg.r stays 255.
Output: 255. KEY: no & = copy = caller unchanged. Final values: bg.r=255, bg.g=128, c.r=0

steps.code: struct Score { int val; int max; };
steps.annotation: STRATEGY: see & on parameter = pass by REFERENCE = same box, two names. Track test.val, test.max
& means s IS test. Not a copy. One box, two labels. Any write to s.val directly changes test.val.
test.val = 85, test.max = 100.
s is bound to test (reference). s.val = 0 writes directly to test.val. Now test.val = 0.
Output: 0. KEY: & = reference = caller IS modified. Final: test.val=0, test.max=100

steps.code: struct Vec { int dx; int dy; };
steps.annotation: STRATEGY: & reference + multiple field mutations. Track move.dx, move.dy through every write.
v is reference to caller's struct. Every write to v.dx or v.dy changes move directly.
move.dx = 5, move.dy = 3.
v.dx reads current move.dx (5), multiplies by 2. move.dx = 10.
v.dy reads current move.dy (3), multiplies by 2. move.dy = 6.
Output: 10 6. Both fields mutated through reference. Final: move.dx=10, move.dy=6
STRATEGY: array of structs passed by &. Track each element separately: boxes[0].size, boxes[1].size, boxes[2].size
& on array parameter = caller's array is directly accessible. arr[i] IS boxes[i].
boxes[0].size=10, boxes[1].size=20, boxes[2].size=30
arr IS boxes (reference). arr[0].size = 0 writes directly to boxes[0].size. Now boxes[0].size = 0.
Output: 0. Only index 0 changed. Final: boxes = {0, 20, 30}

steps.code: struct Song { int bpm; string name; };
steps.annotation: STRATEGY: struct array by & with multiple field writes. Track each field of modified elements.
playlist[0] = {120, Beat}, playlist[1] = {100, Chill}
tracks IS playlist (reference). playlist[0].bpm changes from 120 to 140.
playlist[0].name changes from Beat to Remix. playlist[1] untouched.
Output: 140 Remix. Final: playlist[0]={140,Remix}, playlist[1]={100,Chill}

steps.code: struct Size { int w; int h; };
steps.annotation: STRATEGY: const & = read-only reference. Function can READ but NOT WRITE. Caller stays unchanged.
const prevents s.w = ... or s.h = ... inside function. Compiler would reject any write.
box.w = 10, box.h = 5.
Reads box through reference s. Prints: 10x5. No mutation possible.
Output total: 10x5 10. box unchanged. KEY: const & = efficiency without risk.
STRATEGY: variable copy = snapshot at moment of assignment. Draw two boxes. a = 8.
b copies a's CURRENT value: b = 8. They are now independent boxes. No link between them.
Only a's box changes. a = 20. b still holds its snapshot: b = 8.
Output: 8. KEY: int copy = independent boxes. Changing one never affects the other. Final: a=20, b=8
STRATEGY: draw array boxes with indices. v[0]=5, v[1]=5, v[2]=5
Overwrite v[0]. v[0]=9. Others unchanged.
Read v[0] (currently 9). Add 3. Write to v[2]. v[2]=12. KEY: always use CURRENT value when reading. Final: v={9, 5, 12}
STRATEGY: loop trace = make a table with columns for i and accumulator. product = 1.
i starts at 1. Loop runs while i <= 3. After each iteration, i increments by 1.
i=1: product = 1*1 = 1. i=2: product = 1*2 = 2. i=3: product = 2*3 = 6. Then i=4, fails i<=3, loop exits.
Final: product=6, i=4 (the value when condition first failed). KEY: trace each iteration separately.
STRATEGY: evaluate condition, then trace ONLY the branch that runs. temp = 30.
Evaluate: 30 > 25? YES (true). Enter the if-body. Skip the else entirely.
This runs because condition was true. status = 1.
This line NEVER executes. status stays 1. KEY: only one branch runs. Final: temp=30, status=1
STRATEGY: check parameter for &. No & = pass by value = copy = caller safe.
m = 10. This is the caller's variable.
No &. y is a COPY of m. Two separate boxes: m=10, y=10.
y changes to 50. But y is a copy. m is untouched. m still = 10.
Output: 10. KEY: no & = independent copy. Function cannot affect caller. Final: m=10
STRATEGY: find-max pattern. Init max from [0], then scan [1] onward. Track biggest at each step.
biggest = 2.5 (copy of vals[0]). This is the "best so far" variable.
Start at i=1 (skip [0], already used). Iterate through rest of array.
Final biggest = 8.0. KEY: only updates when current > best. Trace each comparison separately.
STRATEGY: struct + & reference. r IS box. Track box.w and box.h.
box.w = 5, box.h = 3.
& means r IS box. One struct, two names.
r.w reads box.w (5), multiplies by 2. box.w = 10.
r.h reads box.h (3), multiplies by 2. box.h = 6. Final: box = {10, 6}

steps.code: struct stat_double { double numbers[3]; double mystery; };
steps.annotation: STRATEGY: inline max-find on struct array. Same find-max pattern but unrolled (no loop, explicit if checks).
d.numbers[0]=1.0, d.numbers[1]=9.0, d.numbers[2]=5.0.
Init mystery from [0]. d.mystery = 1.0.
9.0 > 1.0? YES. d.mystery = 9.0.
5.0 > 9.0? NO. Skip. Final d.mystery = 9.0. KEY: each if is independent, check sequentially.
STRATEGY: this is the exam Q1 pattern. & reference = data IS d. Track data.mystery through loop. This function finds MAX.
Init mystery from [0]. data.mystery = 10.0. This is "best so far".
Scan [1] through [4]. Compare each to current mystery.
Final data.mystery = 15.3. EXAM PROCEDURE: init from [0], compare each remaining element, update only when strictly greater.
